steps.m: Special letters Ta Sin that open this chapter (exact meaning known only to Allah)
steps.n: These are special letters called 'opening letters' that appear at the start of some chapters of the Quran. They are pronounced as individual letter names: Ta-Sin. While their exact meaning is known only to Allah, they draw attention to the miraculous nature of the Quran.
This is the basic form of the word used to point to something. It's feminine, unlike ذلك which is masculine.

steps.m: that (pointing to something far away, referring to ءَايٰتُ/verses)
steps.n: Added ك with fatha to show distance. This word points to ءَايٰتُ (verses) which comes right after it in the ayah.
Adding ة turns this into a feminine noun meaning 'sign'
Changes from single sign to plural signs by adding ات at the end
Adds nominative case ending ُ to show this word is the subject being pointed to by تِلْكَ (those)

steps.m: to read, recite, or gather information
that which is read/recited (referring to divine revelation)
steps.n: Changed the word structure to create a special noun form that means 'that which is read'. This specific pattern is used for important or intensive concepts.

steps.m: the Quran (object of آيَٰتُ - verses of)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific (the Quran), and ِ at the end shows it belongs to or follows another word (in this case, it follows آيَٰتُ meaning 'verses of the Quran')
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he wrote' to 'something written' by adding long aa sound in middle

steps.m: and a book (connected to مُّبِينٍ - clear/manifest)
steps.n: Adds 'and' at beginning and makes it indefinite. This book is described by the word مُّبِينٍ (clear/manifest) that follows it.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into an active participle (something/someone that does the action)
Adds emphasis to show something that really makes things clear

steps.m: a clear (book) making things clear (describing كِتَابٍ)
steps.n: The -in ending shows this word is describing 'book' (kitaab) in the ayah

steps.m: to guide someone to the right path

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to guide' into noun meaning 'an act of guidance'
The ى at the end makes it indefinite (a guidance)
This word connects to المؤمنين (the believers) later in the ayah, showing who receives the guidance
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Changed the word pattern to make it mean the news itself rather than the action of giving news
Added و at the beginning to connect it with the previous word هُدًى (guidance), showing that both are working together in the sentence
Added short vowels (fat-ha and kasra) to make it mean 'he felt safe' in the past
Changed from a past action (felt safe) to describe someone who believes/has faith as a lasting quality
Added ين to make it plural for male believers
Added لِلْ at the beginning to show this is for/to the believers. This word appears after هُدًى وَبُشْرَىٰ (guidance and good news), showing who receives this guidance.
Added ن to make it refer to multiple people. This is the base word that means 'who'.
Added ٱلَّ at the beginning to make it mean 'those who'. This word introduces people who do the actions mentioned next: establishing prayer (يُقِيمُونَ) and giving charity.
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changes to make 'present tense': Added يُ at start, changed middle vowel to ِ (kasra), and changed ending to ُ (damma)
Added plural masculine ending to show multiple people doing the action. This word is connected to 'prayer' (صَّلَوٰةَ) which it establishes.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed to a noun meaning one instance of prayer, like saying 'a prayer'

steps.m: the prayer (object of يُقِيمُونَ - they establish)
steps.n: Added ٱل to make it specific ('the prayer'), doubled the ص with shadda for emphasis, and added َ at the end to show it's being acted upon by the verb 'establish'

steps.m: to give, to bring, to come

steps.n: Added يُ at the beginning to show 'he' is doing the action in the present. The root letters changed shape to make it easier to say.
Added ون at the end to show multiple people (specifically men or a mixed group) are doing the giving
Added وَ (and) to connect this with the previous action (يُقِيمُونَ - they establish). Together they describe those who both establish prayer and give zakat.

steps.m: to purify or make something grow

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the word to show the act itself rather than someone doing it. The و changes to ا when ة is added.
Added ال to make it specific/definite. The ل gets a shadda because it comes after ال.
Changed وَة to وٰةَ and added َ (fatha) at the end to show this is the object receiving the action of giving
This is a pronoun that refers to those who establish prayer and give zakah. It's connected to the verb يُوقِنُونَ (they are certain) in this ayah.
The وَ connects this to the previous statements about establishing prayer and giving zakah, showing these are the same people who are certain about the Hereafter
Adding آ at start makes it mean 'more/most late' or 'last'
Adding ة to آخِر (last) makes it feminine and turns it into the special word for 'afterlife'
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific afterlife
Adds بِ which can mean 'in' or 'about' depending on context

steps.m: in/about the afterlife (object of يُوقِنُونَ - they are certain)
steps.n: The kasra ending shows this word completes the meaning of يُوقِنُونَ (they are certain) in the ayah

This is a pronoun that refers to a group of people. In this ayah, it refers to the same people who establish prayer and give zakah (mentioned in ٱلَّذِينَ يُقِيمُونَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَيُؤْتُونَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ). These people are the subject of the verb يُوقِنُونَ (they are certain).
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and و between first two root letters
Adding ون shows multiple men are doing the action
This is a basic particle that doesn't come from a root. It shows conditions or possibilities.
Adding the shadda changes this from a conditional word to one that adds emphasis. It makes what follows more definite and connects to the word ٱلَّذِينَ (those who).
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to refer to someone or something
Adding ال with a shadda makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'
Adding ين at the end makes it plural, referring to multiple people. This word is describing the people who don't believe in the ayah.
This is a negative word that makes the verb after it (يُؤْمِنُونَ - they believe) into its opposite meaning (they do not believe). It's one of the basic words in Arabic that doesn't come from a root.
Added a fatha mark (َ) on the ل to show how it's pronounced (la).

steps.m: to trust, feel safe, or believe in
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of safety and security
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Changed from past to present tense by adding يُ at start and adding ؤ in middle

steps.m: they believe (referring to those who don't believe in the Hereafter)
steps.n: Added plural ending to show multiple people doing the action. This refers to disbelievers mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: to come after or be delayed
steps.n: This root relates to things that come later or after
Adding آ at start makes it mean 'more/most late' or 'last'
Added ة to make it feminine and specify it as referring to the afterlife specifically
Adding shadda on the middle letter makes the meaning stronger
Adding نا at the end changes 'he' to 'we' as the doers of the action
Added fatha to the letter lam to help with pronunciation
Added the pronoun هم (them) to show who the preposition is directed to. The damma (ُ) on ه shows it's attached to something before it.
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he did' (past tense verb) to 'a deed' (noun) by removing the last vowel mark
Regular plural form of 'deed' - like changing 'action' to 'actions' in English
Adding attached pronoun meaning 'their'

steps.m: their deeds/actions (as object of زَيَّنَّا/we made attractive)
steps.n: Adding هُمْ shows these are their deeds. This word is the object of زَيَّنَّا (we made attractive) in this ayah.
Added damma and sukun to show proper pronunciation of the pronoun meaning 'they'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence. This word is the subject of يَعْمَهُونَ (they wander blindly) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense, with sukun on middle letter

steps.m: they wander blindly (referring to those who don't believe in the Hereafter)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers back to 'those who don't believe' (الَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ) mentioned earlier in the verse.
This is the basic form used to point to multiple things or people
The alif at the end is lengthened to show distance
Added marks to emphasize the word and lengthen its sound

steps.m: those (pointing to people far away who are mentioned in the next ayah)
steps.n: Added ئِكَ at the end to show we're pointing to specific people who will be described in the next ayah as those who have a bad punishment
This is a demonstrative/relative pronoun formed from الذي (alladhī) with the plural suffix ين and final fatḥa. The shadda on ل is part of the base word.
Added fatha to show this is connected to what follows
Added هُمْ to show this is 'for them'. This word connects to سُوٓءُ (punishment) in the ayah, showing who the punishment is for.
Root meaning 'bad' becomes a noun meaning 'evil thing' by adding vowels (damma and sukun)
Added elongation mark (ٓ) over و to show long vowel sound
Added 'u' sound at end to show this word is doing something (it's what 'they have')
Forms a noun meaning the result of the action of punishing
Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it specific and ِ at the end to show it belongs to something (the 'worst' سُوٓءُ mentioned before it)
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'they' (referring to multiple people). In this ayah, it refers back to those mentioned before who will have a bad punishment.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it, showing these ideas are connected.
This is the basic preposition showing location or containment
The ى is added at the end to complete the preposition. In this ayah, it shows location within the Hereafter.

steps.m: to come after or be delayed

steps.n: The root أخر meaning 'after/delay' becomes 'last/final' by adding ءَا at the start and a kasra (i) sound in the middle
Adding ة makes it refer specifically to the final life (afterlife)
Adding ٱلْ makes it definite ('the'), while the ِ shows it comes after the word في (in)
This is the basic form of the third person masculine plural pronoun meaning 'they'
Added damma marks (ُ) to show this is a separate pronoun that comes before the definite noun الْأَخْسَرُونَ. This form هُمُ is specifically used before definite nouns starting with ال.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added أَ at the beginning and changed vowels to make the comparative/superlative form
Added ون to show this refers to multiple men
Added ال to make it specific. This word is the predicate (description) after هُمُ (they are) in this ayah.
This is a word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. It also makes the word after it end in َ (fatha)

steps.m: indeed you (subject of تُلَقَّى - you receive)
steps.n: The كَ at the end means 'you' (when speaking to one male). This word connects to تُلَقَّى (receive) in the rest of the ayah, showing that you (Prophet Muhammad ﷺ) are the one receiving the Quran.

steps.m: and indeed you (subject of تُلَقَّى - you receive)
steps.n: The وَ at the start means 'and', connecting this statement to what came before it
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter makes it mean 'to cause/make someone do the action'
Added تُ at beginning to make it passive ('being received') and show it's happening to 'you'
Added لَ at start to emphasize that this will definitely happen
Added damma and sukun to make it into a noun form, with hamza replacing the weak letter أ
The suffix ان makes it into a special noun form showing importance and frequency
Added ٱلْ to make it definite (the specific Quran) and َ to show it's the object of the verb لَتُلَقَّى (you receive)

This is a preposition that shows where something comes from. In this ayah, it connects 'you receive' (لَتُلَقَّى) with 'presence' (لَّدُنْ) to show the Quran comes from the presence of the Wise, Knowing.
The kasra (ِ) under the م shows this word is connecting to what comes after it
This word specifically means 'from the direct presence/authority of someone'
The vowels help show this is about someone's direct presence or authority
Adding sukun at the end makes the word stop smoothly
The letter ل gets doubled (shadda) when it comes after certain words, but keeps same meaning
Connected the root letters that mean judging wisely
Added vowels and ي to make it mean 'one who has the quality of being wise'. This describes the Quran mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this is describing Allah as All-Wise, connecting it with عَلِيمٍ (All-Knowing) that follows it
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Adding ي between second and third root letters makes it into an intensive form showing someone who has complete knowledge
Adding tanween (the ٍ) makes it indefinite, like saying 'an All-Knowing one'

steps.m: when (referring to the time when موسى said)
steps.n: This is a time particle that connects to what comes after it. Here it introduces the time when Moses spoke to his family. It's like saying 'Remember the time when...'
Added fathas to show this is past tense and means 'he said'
The و changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the middle of the word

steps.m: Moses (the prophet mentioned in قَالَ/he said)
steps.n: This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew (מֹשֶׁה/Moshe) or Egyptian (ms/mesu meaning 'child/son'). The long alif (ىٰ) at the end shows it's a foreign name adapted into Arabic. In this ayah, Moses is the subject of قَالَ (he said).
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a basic noun
Adds لِ ('to/for') in front and changes ending to show possession
Added هِ meaning 'his'. This word is in the genitive case because it is possessed by Musa (موسى) - it is Musa's family.
Added preposition لِ to show this is 'to/for' his family
Starting with the emphasis particle إِنَّ which adds certainty to what follows
The ن doubles (gets shadda) and takes a kasra sound to connect with 'I' (meaning 'Indeed I')
Added the pronoun ي (meaning 'I') to show who is speaking. Here it's Musa (Moses) speaking to his family.

steps.m: indeed I (speaking to أَهْلِهِۦٓ - his family)
steps.n: Added the elongation mark (ٓ) to show the extended pronunciation at the end of the word
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Adding alif at beginning makes the meaning stronger - from just noticing something to clearly/definitely noticing it
Added تُ to show 'I' did the action, and changed spelling of initial آ to ءَا while keeping the same pronunciation. This word connects to نَارًا (fire) which is what Musa noticed.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is common in Arabic words. This makes it easier to pronounce.

steps.m: a fire (object being seen by موسى)
steps.n: Added tanween (double fatha) to show this is the object that Moses saw, connecting it to the verb ءَانَسْتُ (I perceived/saw) in the ayah
The root letters are changed to show 'I am' doing the action (coming/arriving) right now
Added س to indicate near future

steps.m: I will come to you all
steps.n: Added كُم to show the action is directed towards multiple people being addressed
This is a basic preposition meaning 'from'. It shows where something starts or comes from.
Added sukun (ْ) on ن to help with pronunciation when connecting to next part
Added shadda (ّ) on م because it comes after another word in the sentence
Added هَا at the end to show this means 'from it' where 'it' refers back to نَارًا (the fire) which is feminine in Arabic
Adding vowel marks creates the basic past tense verb form

steps.m: with news (object of سَـَٔاتِيكُم - I will bring you)
steps.n: Added بِ meaning 'with' at the start and tanwin (ٍ) at the end to show it's indefinite. This word is what Musa said he would bring back to his family.

steps.m: or (connecting 'bring you news' with 'bring you a burning torch')
steps.n: Basic coordinating conjunction giving choice between two options: Musa says he will either bring them news OR bring them a burning torch from the fire he saw
Connected the root letters together
Added hamza with fatha and alif at start, and kasra before final letter to make it present tense first person

steps.m: I am bringing to you all (referring to Musa speaking to his family)
steps.n: Added كُم at the end to show the action is directed towards multiple people. In this ayah, Musa is speaking to his family.

steps.m: to flame, to burn with a bright flame

steps.n: Forms a noun for something that burns brightly. This word is connected to قَبَسٍ (burning brand) which describes what kind of flame it is.
Adds tanween kasra to make the noun indefinite and mark genitive case
The بِ at the start means 'with'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create a noun meaning the thing itself (the fire/flame) rather than the action of taking it
Added tanween kasra to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a' flame) and is possessed by شِهَابٍ (flame) in the phrase 'a flame of fire'

steps.m: perhaps/maybe (expressing hope that تَصْطَلُونَ will happen)
steps.n: This is a word that shows you're hoping or expecting something to happen. Here it's connected to the next word تَصْطَلُونَ (you will warm yourselves)

steps.m: perhaps you all (expressing hope that تَصْطَلُونَ will happen)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show this is talking to multiple people. Like changing 'perhaps' to 'perhaps you all'

steps.m: perhaps you all (expressing hope that تَصْطَلُونَ will happen)
steps.n: The shadda (double letter) on ل makes the word more emphatic, showing stronger hope or expectation
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Added form VIII pattern which shows doing something to oneself
Changed to present tense with 'you' as subject
Added plural ending to show multiple people

steps.m: when (connects to جَآءَهَا - she came to it)
steps.n: This is a timing word that shows when something happened. In this ayah, it connects to when Musa's wife came to the fire.

steps.m: then when (connects to جَآءَهَا - she came to it)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happened after something else. Like saying 'then' or 'so' in English.
Added fathas to show it happened in the past. The middle letter ي changes to ا because it's a weak letter in the middle.
Added long alif to emphasize the action of coming

steps.m: came to her (the fire mentioned in النَّارِ)
steps.n: Added هَا at the end to show the action was directed at 'her/it' (referring to the fire)
Creating base Form I verb from root letters
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to make it passive, showing the action happened to someone/something rather than someone doing it
Added the final vowel mark to complete the passive past tense form

steps.m: that (introduces what was called out in نُودِىَ)
steps.n: This is a particle that connects what was called out to what follows. Here it introduces what was announced in the call mentioned by نُودِىَ (it was called).

steps.m: that (introduces what was called out in نُودِىَ)
steps.n: Added fatha (َ) on the أ and a small ن mark (ۢ) to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the word to show that someone or something received the blessing rather than gave it
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to whoever/whatever was blessed in the fire.
Basic preposition showing location or position. In this ayah, it shows that something is located in/within the fire.
Changed the middle و to ا and added fatha to make it mean 'fire' - something that gives light and heat
Added ال to make it specific - 'the fire' instead of just any fire
Added kasra at the end because this word comes after the word فِى (in) in the ayah
Combines conjunction و (and) with relative pronoun من (whoever)
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make it mean 'around'. This changes it from an action (moving around) to a location word (around).
Added ها at the end to show what is being surrounded. In this ayah, it refers to the fire (النَّار) mentioned earlier.

steps.m: to swim, float, move freely through space

steps.n: Changed from basic root meaning 'swim/float' to a noun meaning 'being perfect/free of faults' by adding vowel marks
Added ٰنَ at the end to create a special word used to declare complete perfection and freedom from any defect
Added و to connect this phrase with what came before it. This word is connected to اللَّهِ (Allah) which comes right after it in the ayah.
Changed to the unique form used only for Allah, with ال merged into the word and the hamza removed to show this is a proper name, not just any deity
Added shadda to emphasize the ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added kasra at the end to show possession

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop something to its completion
Lord (one who nurtures and cares completely)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this a noun emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing and care
The kasra (ِ) at the end shows this word is connected to what comes after it (العالمين), making it mean 'Lord of' instead of just 'Lord'
Changed from basic root meaning 'to know' into a noun meaning 'a sign' by adding the vowel marks a-a
Added an alif to make it into a noun meaning 'world' or 'universe'
Added ين to make the word plural, showing there are many worlds
Added ال to make it specific and changed the alif to a long alif because of the writing style in the Quran. This word is possessed by رَبِّ (Lord) in the phrase 'Lord of the worlds'.
This is a proper name that doesn't come from an Arabic root

steps.m: O Moses! - calling out to Moses
steps.n: The يا is added to call someone's attention, like saying 'hey' or 'O' in English

steps.m: O Moses! - calling out to Moses
steps.n: Added damma (ُ) on م to help with pronunciation

steps.m: O Moses! - calling out to Moses
steps.n: Added fatha (َ) before the alif maqsura and an elongation sign (ٰ) to show extended pronunciation

steps.m: O Moses! - calling out to Moses
steps.n: Added small alif (ٓ) at the end to indicate a slight pause in pronunciation
This is a particle used to emphasize what comes after it. The shadda (ّ) makes the emphasis stronger.

steps.m: Indeed he/it (referring to the one speaking in أَنَا ٱللَّهُ)
steps.n: The هُ at the end means 'he' or 'it'. In this ayah, it refers back to the one who is about to say 'I am Allah' in the next words.
The ۥٓ marks are pronunciation helpers that don't change the meaning. They show how to extend the sound slightly when reading.
Added fatha marks to show correct pronunciation of this pronoun
Added an alif at the end to make it mean 'I' in a stronger way. This is how Allah introduces Himself by saying 'I am Allah'.
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Added the U mark at the end to show this is the subject
Added vowels and ي to create the pattern for someone/something that has this quality strongly

steps.m: The Mighty One (one of Allah's names)
steps.n: Adding ٱلْ makes it specific - this is one of Allah's names

steps.m: The Mighty One (one of Allah's names, subject of this ayah)
steps.n: Adding ُ shows this is the subject in this ayah
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into an adjective describing someone characterized by wisdom
Added ال to make it definite. This is one of Allah's names, showing His perfect wisdom.

steps.m: The All-Wise (as a name/title of Allah)
steps.n: Added the nominative case ending to show this is a name/title
Adding vowel marks to show it's a past tense verb
Changed to command form by dropping last vowel and adding hamza at start - tells someone to throw something

steps.m: and throw! (command to throw your staff)
steps.n: Added و to connect this command with what comes before it. This is telling Musa (Moses) to throw his staff.
Connected letters with fatha. The final و changes to ا because it's a weak letter at the end.
Added 'your' at the end. This word is the direct object (what is being thrown) of the command 'throw' (أَلْقِ).
This is a word that shows when something happens. The shadda (double letter) and ا at the end make it specifically mean 'when'.

steps.m: then when (connects to رَءَاهَا - he saw it)
steps.n: The فَ shows this happened right after something else. Here it connects to when Moses saw the staff moving.

steps.m: to see, perceive, or notice with eyes

steps.n: The hamza (ء) replaces the original أ when followed by a long vowel

steps.m: he saw her/it (referring to the staff/عَصَا)
steps.n: Added 'her/it' as the object being seen. This refers back to the staff (عَصَا) that was mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: to shake, vibrate, or move back and forth

steps.n: Basic vowels (fat-ha) added between letters to make 'he shook' in past tense
Added تَ at beginning to show 'it' is doing the action, changed last vowel to ُ to show present tense
This is the basic comparison particle that shows similarity
Adding أَنَّ changes the meaning from simple comparison to a more hypothetical comparison

steps.m: as if it/she (referring to عَصَاكَ - your staff)
steps.n: The هَا refers back to the staff (عَصَاكَ) mentioned earlier in the verse, which is feminine in Arabic

steps.m: to cover, hide, or become dark

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb
Root meaning 'hide' changed to show a creature (snake) that naturally hides - doubled last letter shows it does this a lot
Added a tanween mark (two damma marks) to show this means 'a snake' rather than 'the snake', like saying 'a' instead of 'the' in English

steps.m: to turn away or move in a direction

steps.n: Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels

steps.m: he turned away quickly/completely (subject of رَءَاهَا)
steps.n: The middle letter is doubled to show he turned away quickly/completely, and the ي at the end changed to ى because it's at the end of a word
The dot above the alif maksura (ى) shows this is the final form
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
The مُ prefix creates an active participle (someone who does the action)

steps.m: turning back (describing how Musa وَلَّىٰ/turned away)
steps.n: The tanwin (double fatha) shows state or condition. This word describes the state or condition of how Musa turned away - he turned away while retreating/turning back.
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce this negation particle that makes verbs refer to the past
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it in the verse
Basic vowels (fatha) added to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he returned'
The doubling of ق shows doing the action repeatedly
The يُ prefix changes it to present tense, and the kasra under ق is required with this pattern

steps.m: he looks back (in negative context of وَلَمْ)
steps.n: The sukun at the end shows this is affected by لَمْ which makes it negative past tense (did not look back)
This is the proper name Moses (مُوسَىٰ). The alif at the end (ىٰ) is written specially because it's a proper noun of non-Arabic origin.

steps.m: they (referring to Solomon's armies mentioned before)
steps.n: This is a pronoun meaning 'they' that refers back to جُنُودُهُۥ (his armies) mentioned earlier in the ayah. It's used for a group of mixed gender or all male.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is happening as a result of the armies being gathered. This connects to يُوزَعُونَ (were being arranged) which comes after.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make basic past tense verb

steps.m: he is being arranged/kept in order
steps.n: Changed to passive present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they are being arranged/kept in order (referring to سُلَيْمٰنَ جُنُودُهُ - Solomon's forces)
steps.n: Added masculine plural ending ون to show multiple subjects. This refers to Solomon's forces (جُنُودُهُ) mentioned earlier in the verse being arranged in ranks.
This is a particle that shows something continues until a certain point. Here it connects to إِذَآ (when) to show 'until when' they arrived at the valley.
This is a basic conditional word that introduces a future event or condition. In this ayah, it introduces what happened when the ants arrived at the valley.

steps.m: when (used for future or conditional events)
steps.n: The long alif (آ) at the end makes this word specifically for future or conditional events, unlike إِذْ which is used for past events.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form. The ى at the end is because this is a weak verb ending in ي.
Changed ى to وْ and added ا to show multiple people did the action. This happens because it's a weak verb ending in ي.
This is a basic preposition showing position or relationship. In this ayah, it connects to وَادِ (valley) to show the ants were 'upon/at' the valley.
Added the small alif (ٰ) at the end to show this is a complete word that connects to what comes after it. This doesn't change the meaning, it just helps with reading.

steps.m: valley (a place where water flows down)
steps.n: Root meaning 'flow' becomes 'valley' - a place where water flows down
This word is connected to ٱلنَّمْلِ (the ants) in the ayah, showing which valley is being talked about
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the collective noun for ants
Added definite article ال which causes the first letter to get a shadda
Added kasra at the end to show this word belongs to or describes the valley (وَادِ) mentioned before it
Adding the basic past tense vowels to make it a complete word
Changed middle letter و to ا and added ت at end to show a female (the ant) did the action
Added ة to show this is one specific ant

steps.m: an ant (subject of قَالَتْ - she said)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is the subject doing the action of speaking in the verse

steps.m: to be distinct, to stand out

steps.n: Root letters combined with fatha and shadda (doubling mark) to make question word meaning 'which'
Added damma to show this is the subject
Added ها to make it a calling word
Added يا with extended alif to make it a strong call of attention

steps.m: to move in lines (like ants do)
steps.n: Base root meaning refers to movement in lines, like ants do
Added vowel marks to form the noun meaning 'ants' from the root meaning
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ن because it comes after ال
Added ُ at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence, and changed the initial alif to a special connecting form
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Removed first vowel and changed middle vowel to 'u' to make it a command (like 'you enter!')
Added helper alif with damma at start to make it easier to pronounce the command
Added plural ending وا to make it a command for multiple people

steps.m: to settle, dwell, be at rest

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Added مَ prefix and changed vowels to create noun for place of dwelling
Added alif after second letter to make plural form for places
Added second person plural possessive pronoun كُم with fatha connector

This is a negative particle that makes the following verb يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ (crush you) into 'not crush you'. In this ayah, it's part of the phrase 'so that Solomon and his armies do not crush you while they don't realize.'
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changes to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. This shows the action is happening now or in the future.
Adds نَّ for strong emphasis, showing this will absolutely happen

steps.m: he will definitely crush/break you all (addressing the ants)
steps.n: Adds كُمْ to show who is being crushed - in this case the ants being warned about Solomon and his armies
This is the base Arabic word related to peace from which the name is derived
Added vowels and ي to make a diminutive form showing endearment
Added ان to the peaceful/peace name سُلَيْم to make it Solomon's name, with the alif written as ٰ above the letter
Added ُ to show this is the subject doing the action in يَحْطِمَنَّكُمْ (crush you)
Changes the root into a plural noun by adding the vowels ُ (damma) - makes 'junood' meaning many armies/troops

steps.m: his armies (subject of يَحْطِمَنَّ - will crush)
steps.n: Adds ُ to show this is doing the action, and هُ meaning 'his'. This word is the subject doing the crushing in the phrase 'lest Solomon and his armies crush you'

steps.m: and his armies (subject of يَحْطِمَنَّ - will crush)
steps.n: Adds coordinating conjunction 'and' (waaw al-'atf)

Independent pronoun meaning 'they' (masculine plural). This pronoun refers to Solomon and his armies mentioned earlier, and is the subject of the verb يَشْعُرُونَ (they perceive/realize) that follows.
Adds conjunction 'and' to connect this clause with what came before, showing that Solomon and his armies both 'do not perceive' and were at risk of crushing the ants
This is a basic negative word that tells us the action won't happen. In this ayah, it's saying Solomon and his armies will NOT crush the ants.
Added a fatha mark (َ) on the ل to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'laa'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. This word is negated by لا in the ayah.
Added ون to show multiple people (Solomon and his armies) are the subject, while keeping the negation from لا
Added short vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he smiled' in the past
Adding تَ and doubling س shows someone doing the action to themselves

steps.m: so he smiled to himself pleasantly (before ضَاحِكًا/laughing)
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to show this action led to the next action (laughing) mentioned in the ayah
Added alif after first root letter and vowels to show this describes someone doing the action. This word describes سليمان (Solomon) in the ayah.
Added ًا at the end to show this describes how the action happened - Solomon smiled in response to what was said
Basic preposition that shows where something came from or what something is made of
Added shadda on م because it comes after another word ending in a vowel (ضَاحِكًا). This is just a pronunciation change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Changed from action to noun by adding fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something after it
Added ها at the end to show the speech belongs to her. This word comes after the word مِن (from) in the ayah.
The root letters ق-و-ل become قَالَ (he said) by changing و to ا and adding fatha marks to show it happened in the past
Added و with fatha at beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it, showing this happened after the previous action (smiling)

steps.m: to care for, nurture, or be in charge of something/someone
steps.n: Form I past tense verb pattern فَعَلَ

steps.m: lord/master (one who nurtures and takes care of others)
steps.n: Added fatha on first letter and doubled the middle letter to create a noun meaning 'one who does this action'

steps.m: O my Lord! (calling upon one's Lord)
steps.n: Adding kasra at the end makes it into a form used for calling out to someone
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb form
Adding أ (hamza) at the start makes it mean 'cause someone to do something' instead of doing it yourself
Changed from regular verb to command form by changing the ending vowels - now means 'please inspire me!'
Added ني suffix to show the command is asking for something to be done to 'me', with long vowel at end for emphasis
This is a particle that connects two ideas and shows purpose - here it connects 'grant me' to 'that I thank'. It's like saying 'grant me [the ability] to thank' or 'help me thank'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense and first person (I) by adding أَ at start and changing vowels
Changed to subjunctive mood, used when something comes before the verb that makes it mean 'that/to/so that I thank'
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and change from verb to noun
Changes from a general word for blessing to a specific single blessing or favor

steps.m: Your blessing/favor (object of أَشْكُرَ - that I may thank)
steps.n: Added the possessive pronoun 'your' and changed the ending to connect it. This is what is being thanked in the phrase أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ (that I may thank Your blessing).
Starting with the basic feminine marker تِ and adding ي to make it a relative pronoun
Adding ٱلْ with shadda makes it a specific relative pronoun. In this ayah, it describes Allah's favor (نِعْمَتَكَ) that was given.
The ي is written as ى with a small alif (ٓ) on top for Quranic writing style. This doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Adding أ at the start changes the verb from 'he blessed' to 'he bestowed blessings/favors upon someone else' - making it affect another person

steps.m: you bestowed favor (upon عَلَىَّ - upon me)
steps.n: Added past tense marker تَ for 'you' (masculine singular). This verb connects to عَلَىَّ (upon me) in the ayah to show who received the favor.
Added vowel marks to show pronunciation of basic preposition

steps.m: upon me (connected to نِعْمَتَكَ - Your favor)
steps.n: Added the first person pronoun suffix ي with shadda for emphasis. This shows who the favor is upon in this ayah.
Basic preposition meaning 'upon' or 'on'
Added alif khanjariyah (small alif) to show proper pronunciation
Added و at beginning to connect this word with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Base root relating to birth and parenthood
Active participle form indicating someone who performs the action of giving birth/parenting. The word is in the genitive case because it comes after على (upon) showing who received the blessings.
Added ا after و to make it dual (two), and added َ (fatha) at the end to show these are my parents

This is a particle that connects two parts of a sentence, showing that one leads to or depends on the other. Here it connects the request (أَوْزِعْنِى - inspire me) with what is being requested (أَعْمَلَ - that I may do/work).

steps.m: and that (connects to أَعْمَلَ - I do/work)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it. It shows this is another part of the same request.
Base root relating to action and work
Added vowels to make basic past tense verb
Added أَ at beginning to make it mean 'I do/work' instead of 'he worked'. The word comes after أَنْ (that) in the ayah, which changes its ending vowel.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and kasra under second letter to show someone who has this quality

steps.m: a righteous deed (object of أَعْمَلَ - I may do)
steps.n: Added tanwin alif (ًا) to show this is what is being done. In this ayah, it's what the speaker hopes to do - a righteous deed.
Base root relating to pleasure and satisfaction
Changed from root letters to past tense by adding the vowels 'a' and 'i'
Changed to present tense with 'you' as the doer
Added pronoun 'it' at the end to show what you are pleased with
Base root relating to entering or going in
Added alif at beginning to make it causative - changing from 'enter' to 'make enter'
Added و to connect this command with what came before it
Adds sukun to mark imperative form
Added object pronoun 'me' (ني) to specify who is entering

steps.m: to be merciful, to show kindness
steps.n: Base root relating to mercy and compassion
The root letters ر-ح-م become a noun رَحْمَة meaning 'a mercy' by adding ة at the end
The preposition بِ shows this mercy is being used as a means or instrument
Added كَ at the end to show this is your mercy. The ة changes to ت because it's connecting to another word.
Basic preposition showing location or inclusion within something. Here it shows being included among Allah's righteous servants.
Base root relating to service and worship
Connected the root letters with fatha and sukun to form the basic noun for one who serves
Changed the vowels and added alif to make it plural
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being possessed by something
Added كَ at the end to show these are your servants
Base root relating to righteousness and correctness
Changes the action into a description of someone who has this quality
Makes it refer to multiple people who are righteous
Shows we're talking about specific righteous people, not just any righteous people

steps.m: to search for or notice something is missing

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Doubling the middle letter shows doing something thoroughly or carefully
Adding تَ at the beginning shows doing something with extra attention or effort

steps.m: and he examined thoroughly (subject of قَالَ)
steps.n: Adding وَ connects this action to what happened before. This word describes what the subject of قَالَ (he said) did.
Root means 'to fly' but with vowels it becomes 'bird' - the thing being searched for

steps.m: the bird (object being inspected by تَفَقَّدَ)
steps.n: Added ٱل to make it specific and doubled the ط with shadda

steps.m: the bird (object being inspected by تَفَقَّدَ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the verb تَفَقَّدَ (he inspected)
Added fathas to make it past tense. The middle letter و changes to ا, which is common in Arabic verbs with و in the middle.

steps.m: then he said (following وَتَفَقَّدَ - and he searched)
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this happened right after he searched for the birds
This is a question word used to ask 'what' about something. In this ayah, it's being used by Sulaiman to ask what's wrong that he doesn't see the hoopoe bird.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Starting with the basic combination of ل (for/to) and ي (me)

The ي is written as ى with a fatha (َ) when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning. This word connects to لَآ أَرَى (I do not see) in the ayah, showing who is not seeing.
Basic negative particle that makes statements negative, like adding 'not' in English. In this ayah, it's used with أَرَى (I see) to say 'I do not see'.
The alif is written with a special mark (آ) to show it's pronounced longer. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.
Added vowels to connect the letters. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of the word.
Adding أ at start changes meaning from 'see' to 'show/make see'

steps.m: to hop repeatedly (like a hoopoe bird)

steps.n: Adding vowels to turn the root letters into the name of the bird that hops

steps.m: the hoopoe bird (being searched for by سليمان)
steps.n: Adding the definite article to specify this particular hoopoe bird that سليمان was looking for

steps.m: the hoopoe bird (object of أَرَى/I see)
steps.n: Adding the accusative case ending because this bird is the object of the verb أَرَى (I see) in the phrase لَآ أَرَى (I do not see)

steps.m: or (connecting to مَا لِىَ لَآ أَرَى ٱلْهُدْهُدَ)
steps.n: This is a questioning particle that introduces an alternative possibility. In this ayah, it connects to the previous question "Why do I not see the hoopoe?", suggesting the alternative "Or is it among those absent?"
Added fathas to make it past tense. This shows someone or something existed in the past.
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what group it belongs to
Added fatha at the end to connect it to the next word الْغَآئِبِينَ (the absent ones). This shows he was from that group.

steps.m: to be away or hidden from view

steps.n: Changes the basic idea of 'being away' into describing someone who is away. The ا after غ and the ِ before ب work together to show this meaning.
Adds ين to show we're talking about more than one person who is away

steps.m: the ones who are away/hidden (part of مِنَ group)
steps.n: Adds ال at the start to mean 'the' and َ at the end because it comes after the word مِنَ (from/among) in the ayah
Added vowels and shadda to make basic past tense verb with intense meaning
Changed to passive voice - someone else did the punishing
Added لَ to show strong emphasis/oath

steps.m: I will most certainly punish severely
steps.n: Added نَّ for extra emphasis

steps.m: I will most certainly punish him severely
steps.n: Added هُۥ to show who is being punished

Added vowels to connect the letters and added alif to make it into a noun meaning the result of the action. This word is what will be given as punishment in the phrase 'I will punish him with a severe punishment'.
Added tanween alif to show this is the object (thing receiving the action) in the sentence 'I will punish him with a severe punishment'
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to فَعِيل form to create an adjective meaning 'intense/severe'
Added tanween alif to make it an adverb describing how severe/intense the punishment will be
This is a basic connecting word that shows there are two choices: either punish severely or bring clear proof
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Adding alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Adding لَ at start adds emphasis, like saying 'surely/definitely'
Added نَّ at the end to make the promise super strong, like saying 'I REALLY will do it!'

steps.m: I will most surely slaughter him
steps.n: Added هُۥٓ suffix to show the object (him) being slaughtered
This is a basic connecting word that shows there are two choices: either punishing severely OR slaughtering. In this ayah, it connects the two threats being made.
Added vowels to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense with يَ prefix
Adds لَ prefix to emphasize certainty of the action
Added نَّ for extra emphasis
Added لَ to show this is a strong promise/oath
Added damma and sukun to form basic noun pattern
The ان ending makes it stronger - like going from 'power' to 'complete authority'
The word gets a double line under it (tanwin) to show it's not specific - it means 'a proof' instead of 'the proof'. It's like adding 'a/an' in English.

steps.m: with a proof of authority (object of لَيَأْتِيَنِّى - he will surely bring me)
steps.n: The preposition بِ shows how the proof will be brought. This whole phrase is the object of the verb لَيَأْتِيَنِّى (he will surely bring me)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changes from 'he made clear' to 'something/someone that makes things clear' by adding mu- at start
Added emphasis by doubling the م with a shadda
Added genitive case ending (kasratan) to show this word is affected by a preposition
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added فَ (meaning 'so/then') to show this happened first, before speaking
The root letters get vowel marks (fatha-sukun) to make a word meaning 'other than' or 'different from'
Adding fatha at the end shows this word describes بَعِيدٍ (distant), forming the phrase 'not distant'
Added vowels and ي to make an adjective describing how far something is. This word describes غَيْرَ (time) in the ayah.

steps.m: far/distant (describing غَيْرَ, showing it follows another word)
steps.n: Added the tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this word follows another word in the sentence and is indefinite (not specific)
Added fathas to make it past tense. This shows someone did the action in the past.
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common change in hollow verbs

steps.m: so he said (connects to previous ayah)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement 'he stayed not long'
Changed from root letters to past tense verb by adding alif between first two letters and fathas
Adding أَ at the beginning makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action, and the middle weak letter drops out
Basic relative pronoun meaning 'what'
Added the preposition بِ to show this is connected to the verb أَحَطتُ (I encompassed) earlier in the verse
This is a negative particle that changes the meaning of the verb after it (تُحِطْ) from present to past tense and makes it negative. It's like adding 'did not' before a verb in English.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding تُ at start and changing vowels. The middle و changes to ي in present tense.
When لَمْ comes before this word, it removes the vowel at the end and drops the middle letter ي, making it shorter
Added kasra to show how the preposition is pronounced

steps.m: with him/it (referring to something not mentioned in أَحَطتُ)
steps.n: Added هِ (meaning 'him/it') to بِ (meaning 'with') to show what/who we're talking about - like saying 'with' vs 'with it'
Added a small alif (ۦ) after هِ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
The middle letter ي changes to hamza (ئ) because it's a hollow verb. Added kasra (ِ) and sukun (ْ) to make the basic past tense form.
Added تُ to indicate first person singular past tense ('I' did the action)

steps.m: I came to you (addressing the one being spoken to)
steps.n: Added كَ to show who is being addressed. This كَ refers to the one being spoken to in this part of the story.
Added coordinating conjunction وَ meaning 'and'

Basic preposition showing where the knowledge came from. In this ayah, it connects the word سَبَإٍۭ (Sheba) to the verb أَحَطتُ (I have encompassed/learned), showing that the information came from Sheba.
Added fatha vowels to connect the letters together to form the proper noun
Added the genitive case ending because it comes after the word مِن (from) in the ayah

steps.m: to tell news or give information

steps.n: Adding the basic vowel marks to make it a complete past tense verb
Added 'bi' (with/by) at start and changed ending to match grammar
Added short vowels (fatha and kasra) to make it mean 'he became certain' in the past
Added ي after second root letter to make it into a noun meaning the state of being certain
Added tanwin to show this is describing the news mentioned in the ayah
This is a special word that adds emphasis to what comes after it. It's like adding 'definitely' or 'truly' at the start of a sentence.
Added ى at the end to show 'I' am speaking. The ن gets doubled (شدّة) and takes a kasra (ِ) because of this addition. This connects to the next word where the speaker says 'I found' (وَجَدتُّ).
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it 'he found' in past tense
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action. The ْ (sukoon) helps connect it smoothly.
Added vowels to connect the root letters
Changed from a verb about being manly to the noun 'woman' by adding special letters at start and end

steps.m: a woman (object of وَجَدتُّ - I found)
steps.n: Adds tanween (double line) at end to show it's 'a woman' receiving the action
Adds تَ prefix to make it 'she' in present tense
Adds damma to indicate indicative mood

steps.m: she rules them (referring to her people)
steps.n: Added the attached pronoun هُمْ to show who is being ruled. This refers to the people under the queen's authority.
Connected the root letters together with basic past tense vowels
Changed from 'he came' to 'he was given' - like changing from doing something to having something done to you
Added feminine past tense marker to show the subject is feminine (referring to the queen mentioned as امْرَأَةً in the ayah)

steps.m: and she was given (referring to the queen امْرَأَةً)
steps.n: Added conjunction to connect this clause with previous statement
Basic preposition showing that she was given FROM all things. Links the verb أُوتِيَتْ (she was given) to what follows (كُلِّ - all/every).

steps.m: to make complete, to make whole

steps.n: The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger and more complete
Added kasra (ِ) at the end because this word is connected to شَىْءٍ (thing) that comes after it, making it mean 'every thing'

steps.m: to make something exist or happen

steps.n: Root letters ش-ي-أ get fatha and sukun marks to become the common noun 'thing'
Changed the spelling of ي to ى which is common in Quranic writing but doesn't change the meaning
Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this word belongs to كُلِّ (every/all) in the phrase 'every thing'
Basic preposition meaning 'for' or 'to'. This is a particle, not derived from a root.

steps.m: for her (referring to the queen mentioned as امْرَأَةً)
steps.n: Added هَا which means 'her', referring back to the queen mentioned as امْرَأَةً (a woman) earlier in the verse.

steps.m: and for her (referring to the queen mentioned as امْرَأَةً)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) to connect this phrase with what came before it in the verse

steps.m: to build something high or elevated

steps.n: Adding vowels turns the root into a concrete noun meaning 'throne'
Added tanween damma at the end to show this is an undefined noun. In this ayah, it's described as being عَظِيمٌ (mighty/great).
Added ي between second and third root letters and vowels to form an adjective describing the throne mentioned in the ayah
Added tanween dammah to make the word indefinite
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action
Added shadda on ت to help with pronunciation when connecting to next suffix
Added هَا to show the thing being found is feminine (her/it)
Changes verb root into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Added و at beginning to connect this word to the previous one (وَجَدتُّهَا - I found her)

steps.m: and people (object of وَجَدتُّهَا - I found)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word is being acted upon by the verb 'I found'

steps.m: and her people (object of وَجَدتُّهَا - I found)

Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowel pattern

steps.m: they bow down in worship (to الشَّمْسِ - the sun)

Present tense verb meaning 'they prostrate/bow down' - the ون ending shows multiple people doing the action
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun for 'sun'
Added ال (the) at the beginning, which causes the ش to get a shadda because it's a 'sun letter'
Added kasra (ِ) at the end because it comes after 'and' (wa) in the ayah
Added لِ (to/for) at the beginning to show this is what they are prostrating to

steps.m: from (connecting دُونِ with what comes before)
steps.n: This is a preposition that connects words by showing where something starts from, comes from, or belongs to. In this ayah, it connects دُونِ (besides/instead of) to the previous statement about sun worship.

steps.m: from (connecting دُونِ with what comes before)
steps.n: The word 'min' (from) stays the same and connects words together like a bridge

steps.m: to be lower than or beneath something

steps.n: Adding damma (ُ) changes it to mean 'beneath/lower than'

steps.n: Added ال with a shadda to make it specific. This word connects to يَسْجُدُوا۟ (they prostrate) to tell us who should prostrate.

steps.m: the one who (describes what comes after يَسْجُدُوا۟)

steps.n: Changes from verb (hide) to noun (hidden thing). This word is what is being brought forth by the verb يُخْرِجُ (brings forth/extracts) in this ayah.

This is a basic preposition that shows location or position. In this ayah, it connects 'what is hidden' (ٱلْخَبْءَ) with 'the heavens' (ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ), showing where the hidden things are located.

Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it, joining two things that Allah knows: what is hidden in the heavens and earth, and what people conceal.

Changed from past to present tense by adding تُ prefix and changing vowels

steps.n: Added alif with overbar (ٰ) to elongate the sound. This word is the object of لا (meaning 'no/there is not'), showing what there isn't any of.

steps.m: He (refers to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a personal pronoun used to refer to Allah in this context. It connects to the word اللَّهُ (Allah) from earlier in the ayah.

Added damma at the end to show this is the subject (doer) in the sentence. This word is the subject of the phrase 'there is no god except Him'.

steps.m: to raise up, to build high

steps.m: to be magnificent or grand in size/importance

Added vowel marks (fathas) to make it 'he said' in the past tense. This is the person who is doing the 'we will see' action.

Changed from 'he' to 'we' by replacing يَ with نَ at the beginning

steps.m: we will look/observe (in response to قَالَ - he said)

Connected the root letters with fathas to create the basic past tense verb meaning 'he told the truth'

Changed the word to show who does the action instead of the action itself
