steps.m: we (as the subject of وُعِدْنَا - we were promised)
steps.n: Added the ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence
Root letters أ-ب-و got vowels to make the word 'father'
Changed from 'father' to its plural 'fathers' by adding hamza at start and changing the middle and end
Added damma to show this is doing the action
Added نا to show it belongs to us. This word is connected to نَحْنُ (we) in نَحْنُ وَءَابَآؤُنَا (we and our fathers)
Added و to connect this with the previous word نَحْنُ (we)
This is a preposition showing where something starts or comes from. Here it combines with قَبْلُ (before) to mean 'from before' or 'previously'
Adding vowel marks to make the word readable
Adding damma at the end shows this word is in the nominative case

steps.m: not (negating هٰذَآ أَسٰطِيرُ - these are legends)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it. In this ayah, it's used to negate the statement 'these are legends of the ancients', showing disbelief in the message.
Basic demonstrative pronoun meaning 'this'
Added هٰ at the beginning to show the thing being pointed to is nearby
Added fatha on ذ and extended alif (آ) at the end to emphasize the word, like saying 'this right here!'
Basic form of the exception particle
Adding shadda emphasizes the meaning

steps.m: except, only (in this ayah, introduces what the previous promises were)
steps.n: The long alif at the end helps connect this word smoothly to what follows. In this ayah, it introduces what the disbelievers claimed about previous promises.

steps.m: to write in lines, to record

steps.n: Changed verb into noun for a single line of writing
Changed from 'line' to 'story' by adding 'u' sound at start and changing middle vowel - like turning a single line into a full story
Added feminine ending to specify one complete story
Changed to broken plural form for multiple stories
Added damma ending to show this is the subject after إِلَّآ (except/only). The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this word.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together
The doubled middle letter makes it mean 'first' as a describing word
The ين at the end makes it talk about many people or things that are first. This word is describing the stories (أَسٰطِيرُ) that came before.
Adding ال at the beginning is like saying 'the' in English - it points to specific first ones that were already mentioned
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he said' to a short command meaning 'say!' - like when a teacher tells you to say something. Links to the next command word سِيرُوا۟ (travel)
Adding the kasra changes this to show receiving the action
Adding damma shows this is in the nominative case

steps.m: travel! (multiple people) (followed by فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - in the earth)
steps.n: Added وا to make the command for multiple people. This command is followed by 'in the earth' showing where to travel.
Basic preposition showing location or position
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. This preposition connects with الْأَرْضِ to show location within the earth.
The root letters are given vowels (fatha and sukun) to make the basic noun meaning 'earth'. The word gets a special ending because it comes after the word 'in/on'.
The ٱلْ makes it definite (the earth), and the ِ ending shows it follows a preposition
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he looked' to 'look!' by making it a command - added اُ at start and changed the ending
Added فَ to connect this command with the previous phrase 'travel through the land'

steps.m: and (all of you) look!/so (all of you) observe! (command to group)
steps.n: Added plural command ending وا to show this command is for multiple people
This is a question word used to ask about the way something happens or the state of something
Adding vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly
Adding the final vowel mark to complete the word
Added fathas to make it past tense, showing something that already happened
Changed و to ا because it's a hollow verb (has weak middle letter)
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) but pronounced the same
Changed from a verb (he followed) to a noun (end result) by adding ة, and added ُ to show it's the subject doing something. Works with كَانَ (was) in this sentence.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Forms a noun describing someone who does the action regularly
Makes the word plural to refer to multiple criminals
Makes the word definite and shows it's the object of 'look at' (فَٱنظُرُوا۟) in this ayah
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not' or 'no'
Added و at the beginning to connect this negative statement with what came before. This connects to the verb تَحْزَنْ (be sad) to say 'and do not be sad'
Added vowels to show this is about feeling sad in the past
Changed to 'you' form and added marks to show it's a command
Adding sukoon mark to make it a command form
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or affecting something else. In this ayah, it connects to the verb تَحْزَنْ (be sad) to show who you shouldn't be sad about.
The ى changes to ي before adding pronouns

steps.m: upon them (who are being discussed in the ayah)
steps.n: Added هِمْ which means 'them' when talking about a group of people. This connects to the previous ayah to show who shouldn't be grieved over.
This is the basic negative particle that means 'not' or 'no'
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what comes before it. This وَ connects this negative command with other parts of the verse.
Added vowels (fatha) to make it past tense 'he was'
Added ت at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense
Changed from 'you are' to 'you be' by dropping the و and ُ at the end because لا came before it, telling us 'do not be'
Basic preposition showing location or position
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. In this ayah, it connects to the word ضَيْقٍ to show being 'in distress'
Changes from verb 'to be tight' into adjective meaning 'tight'
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this is the object of the word فِى (in). The tanwin also shows this is referring to any kind of distress, not a specific one.
Starting with the two separate words: مِنْ meaning 'from' and مَا meaning 'what'
When مِنْ comes before a word starting with م, the ن changes into another م (shown by the ّ). This is like how in English 'in possible' becomes 'impossible'
Connected the root letters together with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changes from past to present by adding 'ya' at start and changing middle vowels
Added plural ending to show multiple people doing the action
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Changes to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Adds plural masculine ending to show multiple people doing the action
Adds conjunction at beginning to connect to previous statement
This is an invariable question word (اسم استفهام) that doesn't derive from a root
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. The first fatha shows it's a question word, and the second fatha helps connect to the ى.

steps.m: when (asking about time in وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ - and they say, "When?")
steps.n: Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's pronounced as a long 'aa' sound at the end. This word is part of what is being said in يَقُولُونَ (they say).
This is the basic demonstrative word used to point to something nearby
Added هٰ at the beginning to make it specifically mean 'this' (for something close by). The little line above هٰ (called alif khanjariyah) shows it's shortened from ها.
Added fatha on ذ and alif at end to show this is the subject of يَقُولُونَ (they say). In the ayah, people are asking 'When will this promise come?'
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make it a noun. The fatha on و and sukun on ع show this is a noun meaning 'a promise'
Added ٱلْ to make it specific. This word is the subject being asked about in the question 'When is this promise?'

steps.m: the promise (subject of when is this?)
steps.n: Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject being asked about in the question

steps.m: if (introducing condition for كُنتُمْ صٰدِقِينَ - you are truthful)
steps.n: This is a helper word that introduces a condition - it means 'if something is true, then something else happens.' Here it's saying 'if you are truthful...' connecting to the next part of the sentence.
Added fathas to connect the letters and make the past tense base form
The middle weak letter و drops out and the first vowel changes to damma in hollow verbs
Added تُ to show who did the action in the past
Changed from singular (I/you/she) to plural 'you all' by adding مْ at the end
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb
Changes the verb into a noun describing someone who does the action
Makes the word plural to refer to multiple truthful people
Adds the accusative case ending to show this word is an object
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense form
Changed to command form by adding damma at start and sukun at end. The middle weak letter و drops out in this form.
Basic vowel marks (fatha) added to make the root letters into 'he hoped'
Adding ى at the end makes this a special 'hoping' verb that often means 'perhaps'

steps.m: perhaps/maybe (expressing hope about يَكُونَ - it will be)
steps.n: Added pronunciation guides: the superscript alif (ٰ) and small alif (ٓ) help show how to say the word correctly
This is a connecting particle that joins عَسَىٰٓ (perhaps/it may be) with يَكُونَ (it is/will be). It helps express the meaning 'perhaps it may be that...'
Added vowels to connect the letters and show past tense meaning
Added يَ at the start to make it mean 'he' in present time, and changed middle vowel to ُ (damma) which is common for this type of verb
Added fatha (َ) at the end to show this is in a special grammar position

steps.m: it followed closely (referring to the punishment they were rushing for)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb. In this ayah, it refers to the punishment that follows closely behind those who mock.
Basic preposition showing direction or purpose

steps.m: for you all (connected to رَدِفَ - followed closely)
steps.n: Added the plural 'you' pronoun كُم to show who the action is for. This connects to the verb رَدِفَ (followed closely) in the ayah.
Added fatha and sukun to connect the letters and make it into a noun meaning 'a part'

steps.m: some/part of (subject of يَكُونَ - to be)
steps.n: Added damma (ُ) at the end because this word is the subject of يَكُونَ (to be) in this ayah
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to connect descriptions to what they describe, like saying 'the person who...' or 'the thing that...' in English
Added ال with a shadda to make it specific. This word connects to تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (you seek to hasten), describing what they are hastening.

steps.m: to hurry or do something quickly

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Added تَسْتَ at the beginning to show seeking or wanting to do something, and changed the vowels to show present tense
Added ون at the end to show that many people are doing the action together
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it more certain
Adding و at the beginning connects this statement to what came before it, showing these are related thoughts
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures completely and perfectly
Adds كَ to show it belongs to you. This word comes after إِنَّ (indeed/verily) which is why it has the َ at the end.
This is a special word used to show possession or ownership. Here it connects to فَضْلٍ (bounty) to show 'possessor of bounty'

steps.m: surely a possessor of (of فَضْلٍ - bounty)
steps.n: The لَ adds emphasis because it comes after إِنَّ (indeed) in إِنَّ رَبَّكَ (indeed your Lord), making it even stronger - like saying 'most certainly'

steps.m: to give extra or more than needed

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make the root into a noun meaning the extra good things someone gives. This word belongs to ذُو (owner of) in this ayah.
Added tanween kasra at the end to show this is any favor/blessing in general, not a specific one.
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of, over, or directed towards something else. In this ayah, it connects to 'the people' (ٱلنَّاسِ) to show Allah's favor is upon them.
Changed from root meaning 'to be social' into the word for 'people' by adding an alif (ا) after the first letter
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning, making it refer more strongly to all people
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to something after it
Added ٱل to specify these particular people. This word follows عَلَى (upon) in the ayah.
This word shows contrast with what came before. It's responding to إِنَّ (indeed) in the previous statement to say 'however' about a different point.
The و connects this contrasting statement to the previous statement about Allah's favor, showing both ideas are true
Changed from basic root to past tense verb meaning 'it became many/abundant'
Added أ to make it mean 'more/most abundant'
Added هُمْ to show we're talking about 'them'

This is a negative particle that makes the verb يَشْكُرُونَ (they thank/are grateful) into 'they do not thank/are not grateful'. It's one of the most common words used to make things negative in Arabic.
Added fatha mark on the ل to show how it's pronounced (la)
This is the basic meaning of showing appreciation or gratitude
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing middle vowel to ُ

steps.m: they thank/are thanking (referring to most people in ٱلنَّاسِ)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This refers back to 'most people' (أَكْثَرَهُمْ) from the phrase 'but most of them do not thank'
This is a basic word that adds emphasis to what comes after it, like adding 'definitely' or 'certainly' in English

steps.m: and indeed (connects to what your Lord knows)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. This word introduces what your Lord knows in this ayah.
The fatha at the end shows this word is being affected by the word إِنَّ (indeed) that comes before it

steps.m: your Lord (being acted upon by إِنَّ)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end shows this Lord belongs to or relates to you
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
The يَ at the start changes it from past tense (he knew) to present tense (he knows), while the ُ ending shows it's doing the action

steps.m: surely he knows (what their hearts conceal)
steps.n: The لَ at the start makes the meaning stronger and more certain

steps.m: that which (object of يَعْلَمُ - he knows)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that connects two parts of a sentence. Here it refers to the things that are being known.
Added vowels to connect the letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'he hid' to present tense 'conceals' by changing vowels and combining the two ن letters into one with shadda
Added تُ prefix to show this is about a female doing the action
Makes the action into a concrete noun - the chest, which is where breath and feelings emerge from. This word is what does the hiding in تُكِنُّ (conceals/hides).
Makes the word plural to show multiple chests. The و helps make it a broken plural.
Adds ُ to show this word is doing the action of hiding (تُكِنُّ)
Adds هُمْ to show these are their chests, referring to the people being discussed

steps.m: what (refers to what they make public in يُعْلِنُونَ)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that refers to things. In this ayah, it specifically refers to what the disbelievers make public, connecting to the verb يُعْلِنُونَ (they make public).

steps.m: and what (refers to what they make public in يُعْلِنُونَ)
steps.n: The وَ connects this to the previous phrase about what their hearts conceal (مَا تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ), showing that Allah knows both their hidden and public matters
Adds ي at start to mean 'he' and vowels to show it's happening now/future - yu3linu means 'he reveals'

steps.m: they make public (referring to what the disbelievers conceal and reveal in صُدُورُهُمْ)
steps.n: The ونَ at the end shows a group of people (in this case, the disbelievers) are doing the action. This word pairs with تُكِنُّ (they conceal) in the ayah to contrast what they hide versus what they reveal.
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation. This word can either ask about something (what?) or negate something (not).

steps.m: and what/and not (connects this word to previous statement)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. In this ayah, it's being used as 'and not' to continue listing what is mentioned in Allah's clear book.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it's talking about hidden things that come from the heavens and earth.
Added a sukun (ْ) on the ن to show where the word stops
Added vowels to connect the root letters
Changed from verb (he hid) to noun (one who hides)
Adding ة makes the word feminine
The word takes this ending because it comes after the preposition مِنْ in the phrase وَمَا مِنْ غَآئِبَةٍ (and there is not any hidden thing)
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment. In this ayah, it shows that something is contained within ٱلسَّمَآءِ (the heaven/sky) and وَٱلْأَرْضِ (the earth).
Adding ى at the end makes the word complete and ready to connect with what comes after it

steps.m: to rise up or be elevated

steps.n: Connected the letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the verb 'to rise' into a noun meaning 'sky' by adding alif and hamza at the end
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end to show this word is the object of a preposition
Word is in its final form with definite article, long vowel, and case ending
Added short 'a' (فتحة) and 'sukoon' marks to the root letters to make the word for 'earth'
Added 'the' to make it specific

steps.m: of the earth (possessed by/connected to something)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to something else in the sentence

steps.m: and of the earth (connected to السَّمَآءِ - the heaven/sky)
steps.n: Added 'and' at the beginning to connect it with 'the heaven' mentioned before it

steps.m: except (what comes after this word is not included in what was mentioned before)
steps.n: This is a special word that shows something is different or separate from other things. For example, if you say 'everyone came except Ahmed', this word 'إِلَّا' tells us Ahmed didn't come.
Basic preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it shows that what is being discussed exists within a clear book (فِى كِتٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ).
Adding ى at the end makes the preposition complete and ready to connect to the word after it.
The root letters form the basic concept of writing
Changes from verb 'to write' into noun 'book' by adding alif
Changed regular alif to alif with overbar (purely spelling variation)
Added tanwin kasra to show this is indefinite (like 'a' or 'an' in English)
The root conveys the idea of clarity or distinction
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past-tense verb
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to show something that does the action of making clear
Added emphasis (shadda) to the م
Added tanween to show this is describing something in a general way
This is a particle that emphasizes the truth of what comes after it
Added kasra (ِ) and shadda (ّ) to show this is a strong emphasis particle. In this ayah, it emphasizes the truth about 'this Quran' (هٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ) that follows.
Basic demonstrative element meaning 'here' or 'this'
Added ذا to make it specifically point to something masculine and nearby. The alif at the end shows it's pointing to something masculine.
Changes from 'to read' into 'something that is read' by adding -aan ending
Makes the word specific by adding the definite article ال. This word refers to THE divine revelation, not just any recitation.
The fatha shows this word is receiving the action of يَقُصُّ (narrates/relates) in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
The middle ص is combined with the second ص and marked with shadda (ّ) to make the word shorter while keeping the same meaning of telling/narrating (to the Children of Israel)
Basic preposition showing something is physically or metaphorically on top of or over something else. Here it connects to بَنِىٓ (children/tribe) to show what the Quran explains about.
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end which is just a spelling variation of the long 'aa' sound - it doesn't change the meaning
Adding vowel marks to make it a complete past tense verb
This is a completely different word - not a transformation. بَنِىٓ means 'children/descendants' while بَنِيَ means 'he built'. They just look similar but are different words.
First part of the compound name, from the root س-ر-ي/س-ر-ى meaning 'to travel by night/strive'

steps.m: Israel (meaning: Servant of Allah) - name of Prophet Jacob (peace be upon him)
steps.n: Combined with إيل (El/Il) meaning 'God' to form the complete name. This is a proper noun referring to Prophet Jacob (peace be upon him), who was given this name by Allah.
Added vowels to make a past tense verb showing something becoming abundant

steps.m: more/most (of what is being discussed in the ayah)
steps.n: Changed from 'became abundant' to 'more/most abundant' by adding أَ at the start - like adding 'more' in English
Added fatha at the end because this word is connected to what comes after it, showing what they differ about most
Base word meaning 'possessor of' or 'owner of'
Adding ٱلْ changes this into a relative pronoun. The ل gets a shadda because it combines with another ل sound. This word connects back to 'Children of Israel' to tell us more about them.

steps.m: they/them (referring to the Children of Israel who disagree)
steps.n: This is a basic pronoun that refers to a group of people (masculine). In this ayah, it refers back to the Children of Israel (بَنِىٓ إِسْرٰٓءِيلَ) who are disagreeing about something.
This is a basic preposition that shows location or containment
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'in it', referring back to the Quran (ٱلْقُرْءَانَ) mentioned earlier in the ayah. This helps connect the meaning to what's being discussed.
Added basic vowels to connect the root letters
Adds ا and ت to show people differing with each other (mutual action)
Present tense prefix ي changes verb from past to present
Changed from 'he differs' to 'they differ' by adding ون at the end - shows multiple people disagreeing
This is a common particle used for emphasis. It makes what comes after it more definite and certain.
Added و at the beginning to connect this with what came before it

steps.m: and indeed it (referring to the Quran)
steps.n: Added هُۥ at the end to show what is being talked about (the Quran). The ۥ mark shows this is a long vowel sound.
The last root letter ي changes to و because it's at the end of a past tense verb
Added damma (ُ) on ه and fatha (َ) on د to make it a noun meaning 'guidance'. The ي at the end becomes ى because it's at the end of the noun.
Added tanween (ً) to make it indefinite, meaning 'a guidance'
Added ل with َ at start to emphasize 'surely/indeed'

steps.m: to be merciful, to show compassion

steps.n: Adding ة changes it from a verb into a feminine noun meaning 'mercy'
Adding tanween (double damma) makes it 'a mercy' instead of 'the mercy'
Added و to join this with هُدًى (guidance), showing that the Quran is both a guidance and a mercy
This is the basic past tense form of the verb
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed to show the person who does the action
Changed to show many people who believe
Added لِ to show the word means 'for' and ال to point to specific believers
Added emphasis with shadda on ل and final vowel mark
This is a foundational particle that adds emphasis to what follows it. In this ayah, it emphasizes 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ).
Added fatha marks to connect the letters together
Adding the double letter (shadda) changes this from a verb (to care for) into a noun meaning someone who does a lot of caring - like a lord or master
The كَ at the end means 'your' when speaking to one male. This word is affected by إِنَّ which comes before it in the ayah.

steps.m: to judge between things or settle matters

steps.n: Added fathas to make past tense verb

Changes past tense to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels. This shows the action is happening now or in an ongoing way. In this ayah, it shows Allah actively judging between His creation.
The ى at the end shows this is a special kind of verb that ends with a weak letter ي. This is very common in Arabic.

steps.m: to be separate from one another

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to separate' into noun meaning 'between'
Added هُم at the end to mean 'between them' (referring to males)
Changed the root into a noun showing the result of judging - the actual decision or judgment that comes from the action

steps.m: his judgment (possessed by رَبَّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Added هِ ('his') to show the judgment belongs to Allah. The ِ before هِ shows this word is connected to 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ) who makes the judgment.
Added بِ at the start to show this is how the judgment happens - your Lord judges between them using/by his judgment
This is a personal pronoun used for males or masculine objects. In this ayah, it's the subject of العَزِيزُ (The Mighty)
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. This connects the previous statement about Allah judging between them to the description of His attributes that follows.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past-tense verb
Adding ي in the middle makes this an intensive adjective - showing someone/something has a lot of this quality

steps.m: The Mighty One (a name/attribute of Allah)
steps.n: When ٱلْعَزِيزُ is used as a name of Allah, it means He has complete and perfect might and power
Added vowels and ي to show this describes someone who has complete knowledge
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ُ to show it's describing Allah. This is one of Allah's divine names showing His perfect and complete knowledge of everything.

steps.m: to trust or rely on someone/something

steps.n: Doubling the middle letter makes the meaning stronger
Adding تَ at the start changes it from 'entrusting someone else' to 'entrusting yourself' (to Allah)
Changed from 'he relied' to 'you rely!' by adding a stop mark (sukoon) at the end - making it a command

steps.m: so rely completely! (command to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: Adding فَ shows this command follows from what came before. This is Allah commanding Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to put his complete trust in Him.
This is the basic form meaning something high or elevated
Adding ى at the end turns this into a preposition showing that something depends on or rests upon something else. Here it connects 'rely' with 'Allah', showing we should rely upon Allah.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Added a long vowel mark (small alif) after the second letter to extend the sound
Added 'the' to make it specific and definite
The word 'Allah' comes from 'al-ilah' (the god), but is a special name. The -i ending shows it connects to عَلَى (upon) before it.
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger, like adding 'definitely' or 'surely' in English. Here it emphasizes 'rely' (فَتَوَكَّلْ)
Added كَ which means 'you' when talking to one male person. This 'you' is being described as being 'upon the clear truth' (عَلَى ٱلْحَقِّ ٱلْمُبِينِ)
This is a preposition that shows trust is being placed upon Allah. In this ayah, it connects the verb فَتَوَكَّلْ (so rely/trust) with ٱللَّهِ (Allah).
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb. This word shows who was affected when the Horn was blown (يُنفَخُ).

steps.m: and so he became suddenly frightened (subject of يُنفَخُ)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens as a result of the Horn being blown
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to those who will become terrified when the Horn is blown.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
This is a basic preposition showing something happens inside or within something else. Here it shows the blowing happens inside the Horn.
The ي changes to ى when it comes at the end of a word in the middle of a sentence. This is just a writing change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Adding fatha marks creates the basic past tense verb pattern
Root word for 'rise up' becomes 'heavens' by adding -aat ending to make it plural
Adds definite article to make the noun definite

steps.m: of the heavens/skies (in relation to مَن فِى - whoever is in)
steps.n: The kasra shows this word follows فِى (in) from the phrase مَن فِى (whoever is in)
Combination of 'and' (و) with the relative pronoun 'who/whoever' (من)
Basic preposition showing location. In this ayah, it shows where the blowing happens - in the horn.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and make it into a noun for the physical earth
Added ال to make it specific and ِ because it comes after the word في (in) in the ayah
The word 'earth' has a kasra (small diagonal line) at the end because it comes after the word 'in' (في), showing where something is located

This is a basic exception word that shows something or someone is not included in what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it excludes those whom Allah wills from being frightened when the trumpet is blown.
This is a relative pronoun used to refer to people. In this ayah, it refers to whoever/all those who are in the heavens, and they are the subject (doer) of the verb فَزِعَ (became terrified).
This root represents the basic concept of willing or wanting something
Added vowels (fat-ha) to make it past tense 'he willed'
The weak letter ي changes to ا, which is a common change in Arabic verbs

steps.m: to worship, to be devoted to
steps.n: This is the three-letter root that gives us the idea of worship and devotion
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Added ال to specify this is THE one true deity, and the hamza was dropped because this word is used so often
Added shadda to emphasize that this is the one and only God

steps.m: Allah (as the doer of the action)
steps.n: Added damma at the end to show Allah is the one doing the action in this sentence, and changed alif to wasl-alif since it comes after another word
The last letter ي changes to و because it's a weak letter at the end
Added damma and shadda to create the noun meaning 'all' or 'every'
Added double damma to show this word is the subject of the verb أَتَوْهُ (they came to Him) in this ayah
Added و at beginning to connect this phrase with what came before
This is the basic root meaning showing the concept of coming or arriving
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make the basic past tense form
Changed the ending to و with sukun to show many people (they) instead of one person (he)
Added هُ to show who they came to

steps.m: to be humbled, to be lowly

steps.n: Added vowels to make it 'he was humbled' in the past tense
Changed from verb 'was humbled' to describe someone who is in state of being humbled

steps.m: those who are humbled (object of أَتَوْهُ - they came to Him)
steps.n: The ين ending makes this plural and shows it's being used as an object in the sentence. The alif is written as a long alif (ٰ) in this Quranic spelling.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Added ت at beginning to show 'you' are doing the action. The أ drops out because it's between two vowels. This word is connected to تَحْسَبُهَا (you think them) in the ayah.
Added و with fatha at beginning to connect this with what came before
Added fathas to form the basic noun for a single mountain
Changed the 'a' sound to 'i' and added 'aa' in the middle to make it mean many mountains instead of one

steps.m: the mountains (object of تَرَى - you see)
steps.n: Added ال for 'the' and َ at the end because it's the object of the verb 'you see' in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Present tense verb for 'you'. This word is connected to تَرَى (you see) in the ayah.

steps.m: you consider them (the mountains) (while you see them)
steps.n: Adds feminine plural object pronoun suffix هَا referring to الْجِبَالَ (the mountains). This shows what is being considered while being seen.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changes from a verb (to be still) to describing someone who is still
Added ة to make it feminine, since it describes الْجِبَالَ (the mountains) which is feminine
Added ً (tanween) to show it's describing 'the mountains' - like saying 'frozen mountains'
This is a pronoun meaning 'it' (feminine) - in this case referring back to the mountains mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: while it (referring to الْجِبَالَ - the mountains)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to show this happens at the same time as what was mentioned before - the mountains appear still but while/and they are actually moving

steps.m: to pass or move past something

steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and make it past tense
The middle letter ر is doubled with shadda, and the last ر is dropped. The first vowel changes to damma (u sound) to show ongoing action

steps.m: it passes by (referring to الْجِبَالَ - the mountains)
steps.n: Added تَ at the beginning to show it's about 'the mountains' (feminine singular) in the present tense

steps.m: to pass by or move past something

steps.n: Added vowel marks (fathas) to the root letters to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he passed by'

steps.m: it passes by (subject of the mountains in وَتَرَى ٱلْجِبَالَ)
steps.n: The doubled ر (raa) letter is written with a shadda mark. The word shows how the mountains move in the verse.

steps.m: to pull or drag along (like clouds being pulled across the sky)

steps.n: The root س-ح-ب (to pull/drag) becomes 'clouds' by adding ال (the) and changing the middle letter to a long aa sound. The س gets doubled because it comes after ال

steps.m: of the clouds (describing how the mountains pass by مَرَّ)
steps.n: The ِ shows this word is connected to مَرَّ (passing by), describing how the mountains move - they pass by like clouds
Changed the verb into a noun showing the result of Allah's skillful creation. This word is connected to اللَّهِ (Allah) which comes right after it in the ayah.
Added shadda to emphasize this is the one supreme deity
Added kasra at the end to show possession, and alif became a wasl alif due to pronunciation rules
Base form of the relative pronoun that connects parts of a sentence, like 'who' or 'which' in English
Added ال with shadda to make it definite and emphasize the meaning

steps.m: the one who/which (connects to أَتْقَنَ - perfected)
steps.n: Added elongation mark (ٓ) to connect smoothly with the next word أَتْقَنَ (perfected)
Added vowels to connect the root letters together

steps.m: He made perfect (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: Added أَ at the beginning to show Allah caused something to become perfect. The sukun (ْ) was added on ت to help pronunciation.
The root represents completeness or totality
Added damma and shadda to create a word meaning 'every/all'. This word describes شَىْءٍ (thing) in the ayah, showing that Allah perfected every single thing.
Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of the verb أَتْقَنَ (perfected) - showing what was perfected
The root represents the concept of something existing or being a thing
The root letters get vowel marks to make the word 'thing' - a fatha (َ) on the first letter and a sukun (ْ) on the middle letter, with hamza (ء) at the end
Added kasratain (ٍ) at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb أَتْقَنَ (perfected). The ي is written as ى but pronounced the same.
Adding shadda changes this from 'if' to a word that adds emphasis, like saying 'definitely' or 'certainly'
Added هُ at the end to show who the word 'indeed' is talking about. This connects to the word خَبِيرٌۢ (well-aware) that comes after it, showing that He (Allah) is well-aware.
Added a small و mark (ۥ) after هُ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning.
The root represents knowledge from experience
Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
Changed to the pattern فَعِيل which shows someone who has this quality completely. The ٌ (tanween) at the end shows this is describing Allah in this context.
This is a preposition that shows how something is done or connected

steps.m: with what (referring to what تَفْعَلُونَ you do)
steps.n: Added ما which makes this refer to 'what' or 'whatever' is being done. In this ayah, it connects to the verb تَفْعَلُونَ (you do), so it means 'with what you do'
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make a past tense verb
Changes it to present tense and adds 'you' as the doer

steps.m: you (all) do/make/act (referring to what Allah is aware of)
steps.n: Suffix ون changes it from singular 'you' to plural 'you all'. This verb describes what Allah is aware of in the ayah.

steps.m: whoever (does جَآءَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ - comes with good)
steps.n: This is a word that means 'whoever' and starts a condition - it connects what someone does with what they get in return. In this ayah, it introduces the person who 'comes with good deeds'.
Connected the root letters ج-ي-أ with the vowel marks َ (fatha) to make the past tense verb 'he came'
Added ا (alif) after ج to make it a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
The alif is written with a maddah (آ) to show emphasis and extended duration of the action
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic adjective form
Transforms the root into a feminine noun meaning 'good deed'

steps.m: with a good deed (connected to جَآءَ - came)
steps.n: Adds preposition 'with/by' to show how someone came (with a good deed)

steps.m: with the good deed (connected to جَآءَ - came)
steps.n: Adds 'the' (ال) to make it specific instead of any good deed
The letter gets a different mark (kasra) because it comes after the word bi (with)
Starting with the basic preposition that shows purpose or direction
Added the pronoun هُ to show who it's for

steps.m: then for him (connected to جَآءَ - came)
steps.n: Added فَ (meaning 'then/so') at the start to show this happens after something else. The ۥ mark is just a reading guide.
Changes from verb meaning 'to be good' into noun meaning 'good'
Adding double dammah makes it an indefinite noun in the nominative case
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere or is being compared to something
Added emphasis mark (shadda) to show strong pronunciation after joining with next word

steps.m: from/than it/her (referring to الْحَسَنَةِ - the good deed)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to the good deed (الْحَسَنَةِ) mentioned earlier in the verse
This is a basic pronoun meaning 'they' (for a group of males or mixed gender)
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this word to what came before it. This word connects to ءَامِنُونَ (secure) later in the ayah, showing who is secure.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'from'

Added shadda on meem due to assimilation with preceding nun in connected speech. This is a pronunciation change that doesn't affect meaning. This word connects to فَزَعٍ (fear/terror) to show what they are safe from.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create a noun meaning the feeling or state of terror
The tanwin kasra ending shows this is indefinite (any terror) and is being acted upon in the sentence - specifically what they are being protected from
Added vowel marks to make the basic noun meaning 'day'

steps.m: on that day (when the good deed is done)
steps.n: Added ئِذٍ to specify 'that' particular day mentioned in the context. This refers to the day when someone comes with a good deed, as mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: to feel or provide safety and security
one who is safe/secure (doer of هم)
steps.n: Changed from basic root meaning 'safety' into a word meaning 'safe/secure' - like changing 'safety' to 'safe'. This word tells us about هم (they) in the verse.
Added ُونَ to show this describes multiple people. This word completes the meaning of هم (they) by telling us their state.
This is a combination of two particles: و (and) + من (who/whoever)
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it mean 'he came' in the past tense
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding alif after first letter
Extended the alif to show emphasis/intensity of coming
Doubled the middle letter to show intensity
Adds -ah ending to make it mean 'one evil deed' instead of just 'evil'
Adding 'the' (ال) to the word, which makes the first س double with a shadda
Added بِ at beginning to mean 'with'. The ال changes to ٱل after بِ
The doubled middle letter shows extra force in the action
Added ت at the end to show this happened to a feminine subject (faces/وُجُوهُ)

steps.m: and so she/it was thrown down (refers to وُجُوهُ/faces)
steps.n: Added فَ to connect this with what happened before - showing this is a result
Changed from singular 'face' to plural 'faces' by following the common plural pattern وُجُوه - this is a broken plural form
Added هُمْ at the end to show these faces belong to them
This is a preposition that shows something is inside or within something else. Here it connects to the word النَّارِ (the Fire) to show where the faces are being thrown.
Root letters n-w-r got short vowels (fat-ha) to make the basic noun 'light'
The middle و becomes ا, changing the meaning from general light to fire specifically
Added ال (the) at the start and doubled the ن because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because it follows the word في (in) in the ayah
This is a question word used to ask yes/no questions. In this ayah, it's asking about whether they will be rewarded (تُجْزَوْنَ).
Added sukun (ْ) on the ل to show where to stop when reading
Added يُ at start and changed vowels to show something is being done to someone
Added ون to show multiple people are being repaid
This is a complete particle used to show an exception. In this ayah, it connects to تَعْمَلُونَ (you used to do) to show that people will only be rewarded based on their actions.
This is a relative pronoun that connects to the verb تَعْمَلُونَ (you used to do) in this ayah. It asks about or refers to the actions that were done.
Added vowels to connect the root letters k-w-n together to make a basic verb form
The weak letter و is dropped in the command form
Added past tense marker ت which can mean 'I', 'you', or 'she' depending on context
Added م with sukun to show this refers to multiple people being addressed. This word connects to تَعْمَلُونَ (you do/work) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Added تَ at start to show 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense

steps.m: you (all) work/do (referring to what was done in the Fire)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are being addressed. This refers to what the disbelievers did that led to their punishment in the Fire.
Adding shadda makes this word stronger, changing it from 'if' to 'indeed'
Adding ما changes the meaning from 'indeed' to 'only'. This combination is very common in the Quran.

steps.m: only/just (emphasizing what comes next in أُمِرْتُ)
steps.n: The alif is lengthened to show a pause and connect to the next word أُمِرْتُ (I was commanded)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show this is happening to someone rather than someone doing it

steps.m: I was ordered (connected to أَنْ أَكُونَ - that I be)
steps.n: Added تُ at the end to show 'I' am the one who was ordered. This connects to أَنْ أَكُونَ (that I be) which explains what was ordered.
This is a connecting word that shows أَعْبُدَ (worship) is what I was commanded (أُمِرْتُ) to do. It's like saying 'I was commanded to worship' where 'to' connects the commanding and worshipping.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Changed to present tense first person (I) form. The word comes after أَنْ (that) which requires this specific ending.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic verb form

The root word رَبَبَ became رَبَّ by combining the two ب letters with a shadda. This makes it mean 'Lord' instead of the basic meaning 'to nurture'. It's the object being worshipped in 'I worship the Lord'
This is the basic pointing word that shows something nearby. The alif is written specially (with a line over it) in this word.
The ذِ is added to show we're pointing to something that's close by
The هِ is added at the end to show we're pointing to something feminine. In this ayah, it's pointing to البَلْدَةِ (the city), which is feminine in Arabic.
Added vowel marks (fat-ha) between the root letters to make it mean 'city'
Added ة to specify one particular city and sukun to help pronunciation
Added ٱلْ to make it 'the' specific city, and ِ to show it belongs to 'this' (هٰذِهِ) from the previous word in the ayah
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects a noun to more information about it
Added ٱلّ at the beginning to make it more specific. In this ayah, it describes رَبَّ (Lord) by connecting it to what comes next.

steps.m: to make something forbidden or sacred

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The doubled middle letter (shadda) shows that this was done thoroughly or completely

steps.m: he made it completely forbidden (referring to الْبَلْدَةِ - the city)
steps.n: The ها at the end refers back to the city (البلدة) mentioned earlier in the verse, which is feminine
This is the basic particle that shows ownership or direction
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it

steps.m: and to him belongs (connected to كُلُّ شَىْءٍ - everything)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who something belongs to. The ۥ mark is just to show the vowel extends. This connects to كُلُّ شَىْءٍ (everything) in the next part of the ayah, showing that everything belongs to Him.
Adding damma and shadda creates a word meaning 'every' or 'all'. This word describes شَىْءٍ (thing) in this ayah, meaning 'every thing'.
Adding damma at the end shows this word is the subject of a sentence
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to form the basic noun. The hamza (ء) at the end is part of making it a noun.

The two dots under (ٍ) show this word belongs to something else. Here, it belongs to the word كُلُّ (meaning 'every/all'), making the phrase 'every thing'. The ي changed to ى but this doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels to show passive voice - the action being done to someone rather than by someone
Added و at beginning to connect this with the previous statement
Changes vowels to show this is passive voice (being commanded)

steps.m: that (connects to أُمِرْتُ - I was commanded)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that joins two ideas. Here it connects 'I was commanded' with what was commanded. It's like saying 'I was commanded that...'
This is the basic concept of existing or being
Changed root 'to be' into a command by adding damma (u sound) - like saying 'BE!'
Added أَ at the beginning to show first person ('I')
Added fatha at end to show this is in the subjunctive mood
This is the basic form of the preposition that shows where something comes from or what group it belongs to
Added fatha at the end to connect smoothly with the next word المسلمين (the Muslims). This is a helper sound that makes the words flow together better when speaking.
Adding alif at beginning changes meaning to actively submitting oneself to God's will
The مُ prefix and vowel pattern creates an active participle - someone who does the action
The ين suffix creates regular masculine plural form
The ال prefix makes the noun definite

steps.m: that (introduces what follows in أَتْلُوَا۟ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ)
steps.n: This is a basic particle that introduces what comes next. Here it introduces the action of reciting the Quran.

steps.m: and that (connects to previous statement and introduces أَتْلُوَا۟ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense first person (I) by adding أ at start and changing vowels
Added ا at the end to clearly show this is 'I' form

steps.m: recitation, that which is meant to be recited
steps.n: Changes the verb into a noun meaning 'that which is recited'. The ان ending shows this is something important or complete.
The -a ending shows this is the object (the thing being recited) in 'I recite the Quran'
Adds ال to specify this is THE Quran, not just any recitation. This is what's being recited in 'I recite the Quran'
Starting with the basic interrogative/conditional particle that means 'whoever'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or consequence of what came before. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb ٱهْتَدَىٰ (is guided) in this ayah.

steps.m: to guide or show the right way

steps.n: Changed from basic root to past tense 'he' form by adding vowels. The ي at the end becomes ى when writing past tense verbs.
Adding اِ and ت shows someone receiving or seeking the action for themselves, like the difference between 'guide' and 'find guidance'
The small alif (ٰ) helps show the correct pronunciation of the final ى
Added small alif (ٰ) above the final ى to show it's pronounced like a long aa sound
This is the basic emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before
Added مَا at the end which makes the meaning restrictive, like saying 'only'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Added reflexive pattern to show doing action for oneself
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowel before final ى to kasra
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on second to make it a noun
Added the preposition لِ to indicate 'for/to'
Added kasra at the end to indicate genitive case
Added هِۦ to show the self belongs to 'him'. The ۦ shows how to continue pronouncing the word. This whole phrase tells us who benefits from the guidance mentioned in يَهْتَدِى (he is guided).
Combines و (and) with من (who/whoever)

steps.m: to lose the way, go off the right path

steps.n: Adding fatha vowels to create past tense active verb pattern
When we have two of the same letters next to each other (like the two ل here), they combine into one letter with a shadda (ّ) on top
Changed to a command form by adding ُ on first letter and dropping the middle weak letter و. The ْ at the end shows it's a command.
Added فَ at the beginning to connect this command with what came before it in the verse, showing this is what should happen next
This is an emphasis particle that makes statements stronger
Adding ما changes إِنَّ from meaning 'indeed' to meaning 'only/just'. The آ at the end is just a writing style for ا
This is a standalone pronoun meaning 'I'. The fatha marks make the pronunciation clearer.
The small circle (۠) adds extra emphasis to show this is definitely referring to the speaker. In this ayah, it emphasizes that the speaker is among those who warn.
Basic preposition showing something comes from somewhere or belongs to something
Added fatha at the end to help connect smoothly to the next word (ٱلْمُنذِرِينَ - the warners). This is like adding a connecting sound between words.

steps.m: to warn or alert someone about danger

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at start and ِ in middle to show who does the warning
Added ين to make it plural. The ِ before ين shows it comes after مِنَ (from/of)
Added ال to make it specific and َ at the end because it comes after مِنَ
Changed the word to give a command. The middle letter و is dropped because it's a 'weak' letter that often disappears in Arabic.
Added ِ at the end to connect it with the next word in the sentence

steps.m: and say! (connected to next word الْحَمْدُ)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to mean 'and', connecting this command to what came before it
Root letters ح-م-د turned into a noun حَمْد by adding vowel marks, changing from 'to praise' into 'praise' itself
Added ال to make it specific, like saying 'the praise' instead of just any praise
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the subject/doer in the sentence
This is the base word Allah, which is a proper name and doesn't come from a standard root pattern
Added لِ at the beginning to show the praise belongs to Allah. Added ِ at the end to connect it with الْحَمْدُ (the praise).
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense form and causative meaning (making someone else see = showing)
Added كُمْ to show who is being shown to (you all)
Added سَ to show this will happen in the future
Makes the word plural by adding the feminine plural ending
Adds a short 'a' sound to help pronunciation
Adds kasra at the end to show it belongs to something
Adding هِ shows these signs belong to Allah
Adding ۦ helps show correct pronunciation of هِ
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changes from past to present tense and adds 'you' or 'she' as the doer
Changed to plural 'you all' to match the plural in سَيُرِيكُمْ (He will show you all)

steps.m: you all recognize them (referring to ءَايٰتِهِۦ - His signs)
steps.n: Added ها to refer back to ءَايٰتِهِۦ (His signs) mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: and so you all recognize her/it
steps.n: Added فَ (and so/then) at the beginning to show sequential or consequential action
This is a basic particle that can mean either 'not' or 'what' depending on context. Here it means 'not' because it's being used with the verb تَعْمَلُونَ (you do).
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter (shadda) shows this is someone/something that nurtures completely and perfectly
The damma shows this is the doer in the sentence 'your Lord is not unaware'
Adding 2nd person masculine singular possessive pronoun suffix. This word is the subject of بِغٰفِلٍ (negligent/unaware) in the sentence 'your Lord is not unaware of what you do.'

steps.m: to neglect or be unaware of something

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changed to active participle form showing who does the action. This describes what Allah is not in relation to people's deeds.

steps.m: unaware of (describing what Allah is not)
steps.n: Added بِ to show connection to what follows (عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ - what you do) and tanwin to show indefiniteness
This is a basic preposition that shows what something is about or related to
This word asks about or refers to actions or things. Here it refers to the actions mentioned in تَعْمَلُونَ (you do/are doing)
When عَنْ combines with مَا, the ن changes to م with a shadda (ّ). This combines the meanings of 'about' and 'what' into one word that asks about or refers to actions or things
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
The تَ at the start shows 'you' are doing the action in the present tense

steps.m: you (plural) do/act/work (in response to سَيُرِيكُمْ - He will show you)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are being addressed. This matches with سَيُرِيكُمْ (He will show you) earlier in the verse.
